figures.gloss: The thunder-god restored to the head of most Rodnover pantheons — the carved oak idol at the center of the kapishche, honored with warrior symbolism, oaths and the axe-amulet, as he headed Vladimir's Kyiv pantheon of 980 before the conversion threw him in the Dnipro.
figures.facet: A revival built on the best-attested Slavic god: Perun is one of the few pre-Christian Slavic deities with solid textual footing (chronicle oaths and the 980 pantheon), which is precisely why the movement reaches for him first — the shard rebuilt from its least-broken piece.
figures.provenance: The Primary Chronicle (treaty oaths of 907/971, Vladimir's pantheon of 980); modern cult documented in Kaarina Aitamurto, Paganism, Traditionalism, Nationalism: Narratives of Russian Rodnoverie (2016) and Victor Shnirelman's studies of Russian neopaganism.

figures.gloss: The 'cattle-god' of the chronicle oaths returned as lord of wealth, magic, poetry and the dead — the dark, horned counterweight to Perun in modern Rodnover theology, patron of sorcerers and musicians as Boyan the bard was called his grandson.
figures.facet: Shows a revival adopting a scholarly reconstruction as living theology: the Perun–Veles storm combat many communities now ritualize is not chronicle material but Ivanov and Toporov's 1974 reconstruction — the academy's hypothesis worshipped as myth.
figures.provenance: The Primary Chronicle (oath by Volos, 'god of cattle'); The Lay of Igor's Campaign (Boyan as Veles's grandson); V. V. Ivanov & V. N. Toporov, Issledovaniya v oblasti slavyanskikh drevnostei (1974); modern practice in Aitamurto 2016.

figures.gloss: The progenitor whose name means 'kin/lineage', honored with the Rozhanitsy — the birth-fate mothers — at meals set after a child is born; many Rodnover currents elevate Rod to the all-source of which the other gods are faces, and the movement's very name ('native faith') carries his root.
figures.facet: The gap between medieval evidence and modern rank: Rod is attested only thinly, in sermons scolding people for setting his table, while his supreme-god status is largely Boris Rybakov's maximalist 1981 reading — carried here as the revival's choice, not as ancient fact.
figures.provenance: Medieval sermons against paganism (Slovo ob idolakh and kin texts on Rod and the rozhanitsy); Boris Rybakov, Yazychestvo drevnikh slavyan (1981), whose over-systematization is itself part of the record; Aitamurto 2016 on Rod-centered theology.

figures.gloss: The revived open-air sanctuary — a cleared ground or grove with carved wooden idols, an offering fire and a surrounding boundary — where communities hold their seasonal rites; the communities themselves are often organized as a veche, the old Slavic assembly, deciding matters in open council.
figures.facet: Reconstruction working from archaeology rather than scripture: the round idol-and-fire sanctuaries excavated at sites like Peryn near Novgorod give the revival its template, so the kapishche is the movement's most defensible act of restoration — a floor plan, not a theology.
figures.provenance: Excavated sanctuary sites of the Slavic world (Peryn, Arkona on Rügen for the wider Slavic type); contemporary practice documented in Aitamurto 2016 and in Aitamurto & Simpson (eds.), Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe (2013).

figures.gloss: The eight-armed spinning wheel adopted as the movement's near-universal emblem — read as the sun's yearly turning and worn as pendant and banner; the name means roughly 'spinning wheel', and the symbol under this name is a twentieth-century coinage, not an archaeological find.
figures.facet: The shard's honest crack: the kolovrat is also flown by the movement's nationalist and far-right wings, and its swastika-form draws them deliberately — the political entanglement is real and documented, though ethnographers find nature-religious and apolitical Rodnovers alongside it; the whole is not reducible to the wing.
figures.provenance: Shnirelman's work on Russian neopaganism and nationalism; Aitamurto 2016 on the spectrum from nature-spirituality to ethno-nationalism; the symbol's modern origin is standard in this scholarship.

figures.gloss: The one goddess named among Vladimir's 980 pantheon, restored in the revival as patroness of women's work, wool and spinning, and of the moist earth (Mati Syra Zemlya, 'Damp Mother Earth'); folk custom later folded her into the cult of Paraskeva Pyatnitsa.
figures.facet: The revival's clearest gender gap made visible: Mokosh is the sole woman in the chronicle's list of Perun's company, so restoring the 980 pantheon whole means restoring exactly one goddess among many gods — the source itself, not the movement, sets that ratio.
figures.provenance: The Primary Chronicle, Vladimir's pagan pantheon (980); later ethnographic linkage to Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa folk cult discussed in Boris Rybakov, Yazychestvo drevnikh slavyan (1981); modern cult in Kaarina Aitamurto, Paganism, Traditionalism, Nationalism: Narratives of Russian Rodnoverie (2016).

figures.gloss: The 'giving god' of sun and prosperity, named in Vladimir's 980 pantheon and invoked in the Lay of Igor's Campaign, where the Rus' princes are called 'Dazhbog's grandchildren' — one of the best textually anchored deities the revival restores.
figures.facet: A double warrant rare in this shard: Dazhbog has both a chronicle listing and an independent literary attestation (the Igor Tale's epithet), which is why revived pantheons reach for him with unusual confidence compared to thinner-sourced names.
figures.provenance: The Primary Chronicle, 980 pantheon; The Lay of Igor's Campaign ('Dazhbog's grandchildren'); modern practice in Aitamurto 2016.

figures.gloss: Heavenly smith-father made head of the divine family in much revived theology, built from a single medieval gloss: the Hypatian Chronicle's interpolated excerpt from John Malalas's Chronographia (sub anno 1114), which equates Svarog with Hephaestus and calls Dazhbog 'his son.'
figures.facet: A whole theology raised on one footnote: Svarog appears nowhere in the earlier Primary Chronicle or the 980 pantheon at all — his entire textual existence is this one Byzantine-derived interpolation, and the father-of-the-gods role many currents give him is a modern extrapolation from it, not an independent attestation.
figures.provenance: Hypatian Chronicle, sub anno 1114 (the Malalas interpolation equating Svarog/Hephaestus and Dazhbog/Helios); discussed in Rybakov 1981 and in standard Slavic-philology treatments of the chronicle's borrowed Malalas material; modern theology in Aitamurto 2016.

figures.gloss: The four-faced war-and-divination god of Arkona on Rügen, his idol and cult described by Saxo Grammaticus and Helmold before the shrine's destruction in 1168; the deity for whom Władysław Kołodziej named his 1921 Świętowit circle in interwar Poland, the shard's own founding moment.
figures.facet: The founder-god the roster forgot: the movement's own period note dates the revival's first organized circle to Kołodziej's Świętowit group, yet until now the god that circle was named for had no node — restoring him closes the shard's own origin story.
figures.provenance: Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum; Helmold of Bosau, Chronica Slavorum (on the Arkona cult and its 1168 destruction); Kołodziej's Świętowit circle and its place in the revival's history documented in Aitamurto & Simpson (eds.), Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe (2013).

figures.gloss: Personified winter and death, whose straw effigy is carried to a river or field edge and drowned or burned at the spring equinox across Poland, Czechia, Slovakia and parts of Russia and Ukraine — a living folk custom the revival re-sacralizes as calendar rite rather than reconstructs from broken sources.
figures.facet: Deliberately left without an archetype tag: Marzanna herself does not descend and return like a dying-and-rising god — her effigy is destroyed once each year and rebuilt anew, so it is the custom that cycles, not the goddess who is reborn; forcing a dying-rising label here would overstate what the folk material actually says.
figures.provenance: The Marzanna-drowning custom (topienie Marzanny) is documented across Slavic folklore collections; modern Rodnover calendar use in Aitamurto & Simpson 2013.

figures.gloss: The midsummer fire-and-water festival — flower wreaths floated on rivers, bonfires jumped in pairs, an all-night vigil, the search for the legendary fern-flower — restored from living East Slavic folk custom (Christianized as Ivan Kupala Night) as the most widely kept feast of the revived calendar.
figures.facet: The revival's least-broken rite: unlike the chronicle deities rebuilt from scattered fragments, Kupala survived in continuous village practice under a saint's-day mask, so the movement's task here was unmasking a custom already alive, not reconstructing a dead one.
figures.provenance: Ivan Kupala Night is extensively documented in Slavic ethnography (folklore collections from the 19th c. onward); its restoration as a core Rodnover festival is discussed in Aitamurto 2016.

figures.gloss: A purported ancient Slavic text written on wooden planks, publicized in the 1950s by émigré Yuri Mirolyubov, narrating pagan history and invoking Slavic gods; treated as scripture by some Rodnover communities (notably currents descending from Lev Sylenko's Ukrainian RUNVira) and rejected by others.
figures.facet: The fault line stated plainly, per this shard's law: Soviet and Western philologists (Lyubov Zhukovskaya, 1960; Oleg Tvorogov, 1990) established the text's language, script and grammar as twentieth-century fabrication, not archaic Slavic — so this node records a documented forgery that nonetheless functions as living scripture for part of the movement it is a fault line within Rodnovery itself, not just a claim against it.
figures.provenance: Mirolyubov's publication via the émigré journal Zhar-ptitsa (1950s); philological demonstration of forgery in L. P. Zhukovskaya (1960) and O. V. Tvorogov (1990); the text's contested canonical status discussed in Aitamurto 2016 and Shnirelman's work on Russian neopaganism.

figures.gloss: The modern Rodnover priestly office, its title taken directly from the volkhvy of the Primary Chronicle — the pagan priest-sorcerers who led resistance and famine-time uprisings against Christianization in 11th-century Suzdal and Novgorod; today the volkhv conducts the rites at the kapishche.
figures.facet: A title restored with its politics intact: the chronicle's volkhvy were specifically insurgents against the new faith, so the modern office carries that adversarial charge forward even where the individual priest's practice is purely devotional.
figures.provenance: The Primary Chronicle's accounts of the volkhvy uprisings (Suzdal 1024, Novgorod and Rostov region 1071); modern priestly practice documented in Aitamurto 2016 and Aitamurto & Simpson 2013.

edges.note: The defining revival-of seam: modern Rodnovery deliberately restores the chronicle-attested Perun to his 980 station — a documented reach back across a thousand-year break, not a continuity.

The revived Veles carries the old Slavic name whose *wel- root scholars link with Baltic Velnias and the Latvian veļi (the dead) — a Balto-Slavic chthonic name-line (Ivanov–Toporov), encoded here to the Baltic node since the old Slavic shard holds no Veles.

Intra-tradition: the storm-god against the chthonic lord is the revival's central polarity — ritualized as living myth although the combat itself is Ivanov and Toporov's 1974 scholarly reconstruction, adopted whole.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: communal open-air offering rites rebuilt from medieval sources and archaeology on the same gift-for-a-gift logic, in movements that arose independently but in parallel.

The Rozhanitsy who receive the birth-meal and set the newborn's lot stand beside Baltic Laima at the threshold of birth — the birth-allotment office in the two neighboring families, a classic Balto-Slavic folklore parallel, not proven descent.

The medieval source's own equation: the Hypatian Chronicle's interpolated Malalas excerpt names Svarog as Hephaestus outright — a documented interpretatio graeca the revival inherits as literal kinship.

The same 1114 interpolation makes Dazhbog 'Svarog's son' and equates him with Helios, mirroring a Hephaestus-to-Helios ruler succession the chronicle borrowed wholesale from Malalas's Byzantine chronography rather than from native Slavic genealogy.

Class seam of neighboring folk-continuity revivals: Slavic Kupala and Baltic Rasa are independent midsummer fire-and-water festivals, each kept alive in village custom under other names and unmasked as each movement's flagship feast — parallel survival, not shared descent.

Neighboring earth-and-moisture mother goddesses honored with women's household offerings; the old sources give Mokosh no genealogy linking her to Baltic Žemyna, so the kinship is structural, not attested descent.

Both personify winter's death-phase within an annually recurring cycle, but the parallel is loose: Marzanna's effigy is destroyed and rebuilt each year rather than herself descending and returning, unlike Persephone's own journey below and back.

A twentieth-century plank-text borrows Veles's name and claims his antiquity; philology dates the language and script to Mirolyubov's own era, making this the shard's stated case of scripture built on a demonstrated fabrication.
Intra-tradition: the Volkhv is the ritual specialist who conducts the rites at the Kapishche — restored office and restored sanctuary, the movement's living priesthood and its ground.

Class seam of Slavic and Baltic reconstructionism: both movements revive a priestly title straight out of a medieval chronicle's own language (volkhv from the Primary Chronicle, krivis from Peter von Dusburg's account) rather than from any unbroken succession of office-holders.

The defining revival-of seam: modern Rodnovery restores the sermon-condemned Rod and Rozhanitsy to supreme-god status, following Rybakov's 1981 maximalist reading — a documented reach back, not a continuity, matching the same pattern as Perun's own revival bridge.

dossier.essence: Rodnovery ('native faith') is the contemporary revival of pre-Christian Slavic religion, practiced by communities across Russia, Ukraine, Poland, Belarus and the Slavic diaspora. Rooted in small interwar circles — Władysław Kołodziej's Świętowit devotees in Poland and Volodymyr Shaian's Ukrainian revival — it became a mass movement only after the Soviet collapse, organizing into unions of communities from 1997. Its central claim is that the gods of the chronicle oaths — Perun, Veles, and the rest of Vladimir's pantheon of 980 — are the rightful ancestral religion of the Slavic peoples, interrupted by conversion rather than outgrown.
dossier.cosmos: Because the medieval sources are thin — treaty oaths, a pantheon list, and sermons scolding backsliders — Rodnover cosmology is largely modern synthesis: many currents elevate Rod, the progenitor whose name means 'kin', to an all-source of which the other gods are faces, with Perun and Veles as the storm-order and chthonic-wealth polarity. Some communities structure reality as the triad Prav, Yav and Nav (the ordered, the manifest, and the otherworld of the dead), a scheme drawn from the Book of Veles, a twentieth-century forgery that parts of the movement embrace and parts reject. The dead remain kin: ancestor-memorial feasts continue the old rozhanitsy table-setting and seasonal commemorations of the departed.
dossier.practice: Worship happens at the kapishche, an open-air shrine of carved wooden idols, offering fire and bounded ground, modeled on excavated sanctuaries like Peryn near Novgorod; communities (obshchiny) offer bread, grain and beer through a seasonal wheel that includes Kupala at midsummer and Perun's day in high summer. Many groups govern themselves by veche, the old open assembly. The movement spans a documented spectrum from apolitical nature-religion to hard ethno-nationalism — the eight-armed kolovrat emblem is flown by both wings.
dossier.canon: There is no ancient scripture: the working sources are the Primary Chronicle and medieval sermons against paganism, folklore and archaeology, supplemented by scholarly reconstruction (Ivanov and Toporov's storm-combat myth) adopted as living theology; the Book of Veles circulates in parts of the movement despite its exposure as a modern forgery.
dossier.reflects: The impulse to reassemble religion as ethnic inheritance after total rupture — a faith rebuilt from hostile chronicles and archaeology, whose honest cracks show exactly where evidence ends and modern choice begins.
sources: Kaarina Aitamurto, 'Paganism, Traditionalism, Nationalism: Narratives of Russian Rodnoverie' (2016)
Kaarina Aitamurto & Scott Simpson (eds.), 'Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe' (2013)
Victor A. Shnirelman, '"Christians! Go home": A Revival of Neo-Paganism between the Baltic Sea and Transcaucasia' (2002)
Samuel Hazzard Cross & Olgerd P. Sherbowitz-Wetzor (trans.), 'The Russian Primary Chronicle: Laurentian Text' (1953)

links.cite: Kaarina Aitamurto, 'Paganism, Traditionalism, Nationalism: Narratives of Russian Rodnoverie' (2016)
Kaarina Aitamurto & Scott Simpson (eds.), 'Modern Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Central and Eastern Europe' (2013)
Samuel Hazzard Cross & Olgerd P. Sherbowitz-Wetzor (trans.), 'The Russian Primary Chronicle: Laurentian Text' (1953)
